question.text: A long straddle involves buying both a call and a put at the same strike. This benefits when:

answer.text: The underlying makes a large move in EITHER direction
The underlying remains stable near the strike
Only when the underlying rises significantly
Only when the underlying falls significantly

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A straddle buyer pays two premiums but profits from any large move. Profit = |Move| - (Call premium + Put premium). Direction doesn't matter — only magnitude. **The Trap:** The underlying must move enough to overcome BOTH premiums. Small moves result in losses. **Cross-Exam Context:** Straddle strategies are tested in Series I Chapter 5 and VIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Straddles are typically bought before events expected to cause large moves (RBI policy, US jobs data, elections).
